mn38:0.2: The Greater Discourse on the Destruction of *Taṇhā*
mn38:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn38:10.1: “From uncertainty, bhikkhus, does doubt arise: ‘Has this come to be or not?’”
mn38:10.3: “From uncertainty, bhikkhus, does doubt arise: ‘Has this come to be from nutriment or not?’”
mn38:10.5: “From uncertainty, bhikkhus, does doubt arise: ‘With the cessation of that nutriment, is what has come to be of a nature to cease or not?’”
mn38:11.1: “For one seeing with proper discernment [*paññā*] as it truly is, bhikkhus, is the doubt removed concerning ‘This has come to be’?”
mn38:11.3: “For one seeing with proper discernment [*paññā*] as it truly is, bhikkhus, is the doubt removed concerning ‘This has come to be from nutriment’?”
mn38:11.5: “For one seeing with proper discernment [*paññā*] as it truly is, bhikkhus, is the doubt removed concerning ‘With the cessation of that nutriment, what has come to be is of a nature to cease’?”
mn38:12.1: “Then, bhikkhus, are you free from doubt here thus: ‘This has come to be’?”
mn38:12.3: “Are you free from doubt here thus: ‘This has come to be from nutriment’?”
mn38:12.5: “Are you free from doubt here thus: ‘With the cessation of that nutriment, what has come to be is of a nature to cease’?”
mn38:13.1: “Bhikkhus, has ‘This has come to be’ been well seen by you with proper discernment [*paññā*] as it truly is?”
mn38:13.3: “Has ‘This has come to be from nutriment’ been well seen by you with proper discernment [*paññā*] as it truly is?”
mn38:13.5: “Has ‘With the cessation of that nutriment, what has come to be is of a nature to cease’ been well seen by you with proper discernment [*paññā*] as it truly is?”
mn38:14.1: “Bhikkhus, if you were to cling to this view, so pure and so bright, to play with it, treasure it, and make it your own, would you then understand the *dhamma* compared to a raft, taught for crossing over and not for grasping?”
mn38:14.3: “But if you were not to cling to this view, so pure and so bright, nor to play with it, treasure it, or make it your own, would you then understand the *dhamma* compared to a raft, taught for crossing over and not for grasping?”
mn38:15.1: “Bhikkhus, these are the four nutriments, for the maintenance of beings that have come to be, or for the support of those seeking *bhava*.
mn38:15.3: Physical food, coarse or subtle; contact is the second; mental intention the third; consciousness [*viññāṇa*] the fourth.
mn38:16.1: And these four nutriments, bhikkhus—what is their source, what is their arising, from what are they born, from what do they originate?
mn38:16.10: The dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] has *nāmarūpa* as its source, *nāmarūpa* as its arising, is born from *nāmarūpa*, and originates from *nāmarūpa*.
mn38:16.11: And this *nāmarūpa*, bhikkhus—what is its source, what is its arising, from what is it born, from what does it originate?
mn38:16.12: *Nāmarūpa* has consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as its source, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as its arising, is born from consciousness [*viññāṇa*], and originates from consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn38:16.13: And this consciousness [*viññāṇa*], bhikkhus—what is its source, what is its arising, from what is it born, from what does it originate?
mn38:16.14: Consciousness [*viññāṇa*] has *saṅkhāra* as its source, *saṅkhāra* as its arising, is born from *saṅkhāra*, and originates from *saṅkhāra*.
mn38:16.15: And these *saṅkhāras*, bhikkhus—what is their source, what is their arising, from what are they born, from what do they originate?
mn38:16.16: *Saṅkhāras* have blindness [*avijjā*] as their source, blindness [*avijjā*] as their arising, are born from blindness [*avijjā*], and originate from blindness [*avijjā*].
mn38:16.2: These four nutriments have *taṇhā* as their source, *taṇhā* as their arising, are born from *taṇhā*, and originate from *taṇhā*.
mn38:16.3: And this *taṇhā*, bhikkhus—what is its source, what is its arising, from what is it born, from what does it originate?
mn38:16.4: *Taṇhā* has *vedanā* as its source, *vedanā* as its arising, is born from *vedanā*, and originates from *vedanā*.
mn38:16.5: And this *vedanā*, bhikkhus—what is its source, what is its arising, from what is it born, from what does it originate?
mn38:16.6: *Vedanā* has contact as its source, contact as its arising, is born from contact, and originates from contact.
mn38:16.7: And this contact, bhikkhus—what is its source, what is its arising, from what is it born, from what does it originate?
mn38:16.8: Contact has the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as its source, the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as its arising, is born from the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], and originates from the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*].
mn38:16.9: And this dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], bhikkhus—what is its source, what is its arising, from what is it born, from what does it originate?
mn38:17.11: with birth as condition, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, distress, and despair come to be.
mn38:17.12: That is how there is the arising of this whole mass of *dukkha*.
mn38:17.4: with *nāmarūpa* as condition, the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*];
mn38:17.5: with the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as condition, contact;
mn38:18.1: “It was said: ‘With birth as condition, old age and death.’
mn38:18.12: Now is *bhava* conditioned by clinging/feeding [*upādāna*], bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:18.13: “With clinging/feeding [*upādāna*] as condition, bhante, there is *bhava*.
mn38:18.14: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:18.16: “It was said: ‘With *taṇhā* as condition, clinging/feeding [*upādāna*].’ Now is clinging/feeding [*upādāna*] conditioned by *taṇhā*, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:18.17: “With *taṇhā* as condition, bhante, there is clinging/feeding [*upādāna*].
mn38:18.18: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:18.2: Now is old age and death conditioned by birth, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:18.21: Now is *taṇhā* conditioned by *vedanā*, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:18.23: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:18.26: Now is *vedanā* conditioned by contact, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:18.27: “With contact as condition, bhante, there is *vedanā*.
mn38:18.28: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:18.3: “With birth as condition, bhante, there is old age and death.
mn38:18.30: “It was said: ‘With the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as condition, contact.’
mn38:18.31: Now is contact conditioned by the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:18.32: “With the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as condition, bhante, there is contact.
mn38:18.33: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:18.34: with the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as condition, contact.”
mn38:18.35: “It was said: ‘With *nāmarūpa* as condition, the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*].’
mn38:18.36: Now is the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] conditioned by *nāmarūpa*, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:18.37: “With *nāmarūpa* as condition, bhante, there is the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*].
mn38:18.38: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:18.39: with *nāmarūpa* as condition, the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*].”
mn38:18.4: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:18.41: Now is *nāmarūpa* conditioned by consciousness [*viññāṇa*], bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:18.42: “With consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as condition, bhante, there is *nāmarūpa*.
mn38:18.43: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:18.46: Now is consciousness [*viññāṇa*] conditioned by *saṅkhāras*, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:18.47: “With *saṅkhāras* as condition, bhante, there is consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn38:18.48: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:18.5: with birth as condition, old age and death.”
mn38:18.51: Now are *saṅkhāras* conditioned by blindness [*avijjā*], bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:18.52: “With blindness [*avijjā*] as condition, bhante, there are *saṅkhāras*.
mn38:18.53: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:18.7: Now is birth conditioned by *bhava*, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:18.8: “With *bhava* as condition, bhante, there is birth.
mn38:18.9: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:19.14: with birth as condition, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, distress, and despair come to be.
mn38:19.15: That is how there is the arising of this whole mass of *dukkha*.
mn38:19.2: So, bhikkhus, you also say this, and I also say this:
mn38:19.3: when this exists, that comes to be; with the arising of this, that arises, that is:
mn38:19.7: with *nāmarūpa* as condition, the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*];
mn38:19.8: with the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as condition, contact;
mn38:2.1: Now at that time, for a bhikkhu named Sāti, the fisherman’s son, this harmful view had arisen:
mn38:2.2: “As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, not another.”
mn38:20.1: But with the remainderless fading away and cessation of blindness [*avijjā*] comes the cessation of *saṅkhāras*;
mn38:20.10: with the cessation of *bhava*, the cessation of birth;
mn38:20.11: with the cessation of birth, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, distress, and despair cease.
mn38:20.12: That is how there is the cessation of this whole mass of *dukkha*.
mn38:20.2: with the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*];
mn38:20.3: with the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], the cessation of *nāmarūpa*;
mn38:20.4: with the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*];
mn38:20.5: with the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], the cessation of contact;
mn38:20.6: with the cessation of contact, the cessation of *vedanā*;
mn38:20.7: with the cessation of *vedanā*, the cessation of *taṇhā*;
mn38:20.8: with the cessation of *taṇhā*, the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*];
mn38:20.9: with the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*], the cessation of *bhava*;
mn38:21.1: “It was said: ‘With the cessation of birth, the cessation of old age and death.’
mn38:21.10: with the cessation of *bhava*, the cessation of birth.”
mn38:21.11: “It was said: ‘With the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*], the cessation of *bhava*.’
mn38:21.12: Now does the cessation of *bhava* come from the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*], bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:21.13: “With the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*], bhante, there is the cessation of *bhava*.
mn38:21.14: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:21.15: with the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*], the cessation of *bhava*.”
mn38:21.16: “It was said: ‘With the cessation of *taṇhā*, the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*].’
mn38:21.17: Now does the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*] come from the cessation of *taṇhā*, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:21.18: “With the cessation of *taṇhā*, bhante, there is the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*].
mn38:21.19: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:21.2: Now does the cessation of old age and death come from the cessation of birth, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:21.20: with the cessation of *taṇhā*, the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*].”
mn38:21.21: “It was said: ‘With the cessation of *vedanā*, the cessation of *taṇhā*.’
mn38:21.22: Now does the cessation of *taṇhā* come from the cessation of *vedanā*, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:21.23: “With the cessation of *vedanā*, bhante, there is the cessation of *taṇhā*.
mn38:21.24: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:21.25: with the cessation of *vedanā*, the cessation of *taṇhā*.”
mn38:21.26: “It was said: ‘With the cessation of contact, the cessation of *vedanā*.’
mn38:21.27: Now does the cessation of *vedanā* come from the cessation of contact, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:21.28: “With the cessation of contact, bhante, there is the cessation of *vedanā*.
mn38:21.29: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:21.3: “With the cessation of birth, bhante, there is the cessation of old age and death.
mn38:21.30: with the cessation of contact, the cessation of *vedanā*.”
mn38:21.31: “It was said: ‘With the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], the cessation of contact.’
mn38:21.32: Now does the cessation of contact come from the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:21.33: “With the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], bhante, there is the cessation of contact.
mn38:21.34: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:21.35: with the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], the cessation of contact.”
mn38:21.36: “It was said: ‘With the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*].’
mn38:21.37: Now does the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] come from the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:21.38: “With the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, bhante, there is the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*].
mn38:21.39: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:21.4: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:21.40: with the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*].”
mn38:21.41: “It was said: ‘With the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], the cessation of *nāmarūpa*.’
mn38:21.42: Now does the cessation of *nāmarūpa* come from the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:21.43: “With the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], bhante, there is the cessation of *nāmarūpa*.
mn38:21.44: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:21.45: with the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], the cessation of *nāmarūpa*.”
mn38:21.46: “It was said: ‘With the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].’
mn38:21.47: Now does the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*] come from the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:21.48: “With the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, bhante, there is the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn38:21.49: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:21.5: with the cessation of birth, the cessation of old age and death.”
mn38:21.50: with the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].”
mn38:21.51: “It was said: ‘With the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], the cessation of *saṅkhāras*.’
mn38:21.52: Now does the cessation of *saṅkhāras* come from the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:21.53: “With the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], bhante, there is the cessation of *saṅkhāras*.
mn38:21.54: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:21.55: with the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], the cessation of *saṅkhāras*.”
mn38:21.6: “It was said: ‘With the cessation of *bhava*, the cessation of birth.’
mn38:21.7: Now does the cessation of birth come from the cessation of *bhava*, bhikkhus, or not? How does this stand here?”
mn38:21.8: “With the cessation of *bhava*, bhante, there is the cessation of birth.
mn38:21.9: That is how it stands for us here:
mn38:22.10: with the cessation of *vedanā*, the cessation of *taṇhā*;
mn38:22.11: with the cessation of *taṇhā*, the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*];
mn38:22.12: with the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*], the cessation of *bhava*;
mn38:22.13: with the cessation of *bhava*, the cessation of birth;
mn38:22.14: with the cessation of birth, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, distress, and despair cease.
mn38:22.15: That is how there is the cessation of this whole mass of *dukkha*.
mn38:22.2: So, bhikkhus, you also say this, and I also say this:
mn38:22.3: when this is not, that does not come to be; with the cessation of this, that ceases, that is:
mn38:22.4: with the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], the cessation of *saṅkhāras*;
mn38:22.5: with the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*];
mn38:22.6: with the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], the cessation of *nāmarūpa*;
mn38:22.7: with the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*];
mn38:22.8: with the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], the cessation of contact;
mn38:22.9: with the cessation of contact, the cessation of *vedanā*;
mn38:23.1: “Bhikkhus, knowing and seeing thus, would you run back to the past:
mn38:23.2: ‘Were we in the past? Were we not in the past? What were we in the past? How were we in the past? Having been what, what were we in the past?’”
mn38:23.4: “Bhikkhus, knowing and seeing thus, would you run forward to the future:
mn38:23.5: ‘Will we be in the future? Will we not be in the future? What will we be in the future? How will we be in the future? Having become what, what will we be in the future?’”
mn38:24.1: “Bhikkhus, knowing and seeing thus, would you be inwardly perplexed about the present:
mn38:24.10: “Bhikkhus, knowing and seeing thus, would you point to another teacher?”
mn38:24.12: “Bhikkhus, knowing and seeing thus, would you return to taking as essential those various observances, agitations, and auspicious rites of ordinary ascetics and brahmins?”
mn38:24.14: “Is it not the case, bhikkhus, that you speak only of what you yourselves have known, seen, and understood?”
mn38:24.2: ‘Am I? Am I not? What am I? How am I? Where has this being come from, and where will it go?’”
mn38:24.4: “Bhikkhus, knowing and seeing thus, would you say:
mn38:24.5: ‘The teacher is respected by us, and out of respect for the teacher we speak thus’?”
mn38:24.7: “Bhikkhus, knowing and seeing thus, would you say:
mn38:24.8: ‘The ascetic says so, and we, being ascetics, say so too’?”
mn38:25.1: “Sādhu, bhikkhus. You have been led by me, bhikkhus, by this *dhamma* visible here and now, timeless, inviting inspection, leading inward, to be personally known by the wise.
mn38:25.2: ‘This *dhamma*, bhikkhus, is visible here and now, timeless, inviting inspection, leading inward, to be personally known by the wise’—
mn38:25.3: it is in dependence on this that this was said.
mn38:26.1: “But, bhikkhus, it is through the coming together of three things that there is a descent of the embryo.
mn38:26.2: Here, mother and father come together, but the mother is not fertile and a *gandhabba* is not present; then there is still no descent of the embryo.
mn38:26.3: Here, mother and father come together, and the mother is fertile, but a *gandhabba* is not present; then there is still no descent of the embryo.
mn38:26.4: But when, bhikkhus, mother and father come together, the mother is fertile, and a *gandhabba* is present—through the coming together of these three there is a descent of the embryo.
mn38:27.1: Then, bhikkhus, the mother carries him in her womb for nine or ten months, with great anxiety, as a heavy burden.
mn38:27.2: Then, bhikkhus, after nine or ten months, the mother gives birth to him, with great anxiety, as a heavy burden.
mn38:27.3: And when he is born, she nourishes him with her own blood.
mn38:27.4: For, bhikkhus, in the noble *vinaya*, a mother’s milk counts as blood.
mn38:28.1: Then, bhikkhus, as that boy grows up and his *indriyas* mature,
mn38:28.2: he plays the kinds of games that children play, such as vaṅkaka, ghaṭika, mokkhacika, ciṅgulaka, pattāḷhaka, little chariots, and little bows.
mn38:29.1: Then, bhikkhus, as that boy grows up and his *indriyas* mature,
mn38:29.2: he amuses himself, furnished and endowed with the five strands of sensual pleasure—
mn38:29.3: forms cognizable by the eye, desirable, lovely, pleasing, agreeable, connected with sensuality, enticing;
mn38:29.7: tangibles cognizable by the body, desirable, lovely, pleasing, agreeable, connected with sensuality, enticing.
mn38:3.10: In many ways, friend Sāti, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] has been spoken of by the *Bhagavā* as *paṭiccasamuppāda*'ed [*paṭiccasamuppannaṁ*]; apart from a condition, there is no arising of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].”
mn38:3.11: But even though the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, was being questioned, pressed, and spoken to by those bhikkhus, he still kept insisting on that very harmful view, grasping at it stubbornly and clinging to it:
mn38:3.12: “That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, not another.”
mn38:3.2: “It seems that for the bhikkhu named Sāti, the fisherman’s son, this harmful view has arisen:
mn38:3.3: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, not another.’”
mn38:3.4: Then those bhikkhus went to the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son. Having gone to him, they said this to him:
mn38:3.5: “Is it true, friend Sāti, that this harmful view has arisen for you:
mn38:3.6: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, not another’?”
mn38:3.7: “That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, not another.”
mn38:3.8: Then those bhikkhus, wanting to draw the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, away from this harmful view, questioned him, pressed him, and spoke to him:
mn38:3.9: “Do not say that, friend Sāti. Do not misrepresent the *Bhagavā*, for it is not good to misrepresent the *Bhagavā*. The *Bhagavā* would not speak in that way.
mn38:30.1: Having seen a form with the eye, he lusts after an attractive form and is repelled by an unattractive form, and he dwells with *sati* of the body unestablished [*anupaṭṭhitakāyasati*], with a limited mind.
mn38:30.10: having touched a tangible with the body …
mn38:30.11: having cognized a *dhamma* with the mind, he lusts after an attractive *dhamma* and is repelled by an unattractive *dhamma*, and he dwells with *sati* of the body unestablished [*anupaṭṭhitakāyasati*], with a limited mind.
mn38:30.12: And he does not understand as it truly is that liberation of mind and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*] where those evil unskillful *dhammas* cease without remainder for him.
mn38:30.13: Having thus entered upon favoring and opposing, whatever *vedanā* he feels—pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant—he delights in that *vedanā*, welcomes it, and remains holding to it.
mn38:30.14: As he delights in that *vedanā*, welcomes it, and remains holding to it, delight arises in him.
mn38:30.15: Whatever delight there is in *vedanās*, that is clinging/feeding [*upādāna*]. With clinging/feeding [*upādāna*] as condition there is *bhava*; with *bhava* as condition, birth; with birth as condition, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, distress, and despair come to be.
mn38:30.16: That is how there is the arising of this whole mass of *dukkha*.
mn38:30.2: And he does not understand as it truly is that liberation of mind and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*] where those evil unskillful *dhammas* cease without remainder for him.
mn38:30.3: Having thus entered upon favoring and opposing, whatever *vedanā* he feels—pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant—he delights in that *vedanā*, welcomes it, and remains holding to it.
mn38:30.4: As he delights in that *vedanā*, welcomes it, and remains holding to it, delight arises in him.
mn38:30.5: Whatever delight there is in *vedanās*, that is clinging/feeding [*upādāna*]. With clinging/feeding [*upādāna*] as condition there is *bhava*; with *bhava* as condition, birth; with birth as condition, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, distress, and despair come to be.
mn38:30.6: That is how there is the arising of this whole mass of *dukkha*.
mn38:30.7: Having heard a sound with the ear …
mn38:30.8: having smelled an odor with the nose …
mn38:30.9: having tasted a flavor with the tongue …
mn38:31.1: “Here, bhikkhus, a Tathāgata arises in the world, an *arahant*, a perfectly awakened one, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, gone to a good destination, knower of the world, unsurpassed trainer of persons to be tamed, teacher of devatās and humans, awakened, the *Bhagavā*.
mn38:31.2: He declares this world—with its devatās, its Māras, its Brahmās, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its devatās and humans—having directly known and realized it for himself.
mn38:31.3: He teaches the *dhamma* that is good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, with meaning and phrasing;
mn38:31.4: he reveals a holy life that is entirely complete and pure.
mn38:32.1: A householder, or a householder’s son, or one born in some other family, hears that *dhamma*.
mn38:32.2: Having heard that *dhamma*, he gains confidence in the Tathāgata.
mn38:32.3: Endowed with that gained confidence, he reflects thus:
mn38:32.4: ‘Life at home is crowded, a dusty path; going forth is open air.
mn38:32.5: It is not easy, while living in a house, to lead the holy life utterly complete, utterly pure, polished like a shell.
mn38:32.6: What if I were to shave off hair and beard, put on ochre robes, and go forth from the home life into homelessness?’
mn38:32.7: After some time, giving up a small or a large mass of wealth, giving up a small or a large circle of relatives, he shaves off hair and beard, puts on ochre robes, and goes forth from the home life into homelessness.
mn38:33.1: Having thus gone forth, he undertakes the training and livelihood of bhikkhus: giving up killing living beings, he abstains from killing living beings; with rod and weapon laid aside, conscientious, kindly, he dwells compassionate for the welfare of all living beings.
mn38:33.10: He abstains from dancing, singing, music, and shows,
mn38:33.11: he abstains from garlands, perfumes, ointments, adornments, and embellishments,
mn38:33.12: he abstains from high and luxurious beds,
mn38:33.13: he abstains from accepting gold and silver,
mn38:33.14: he abstains from accepting raw grain,
mn38:33.15: he abstains from accepting raw meat,
mn38:33.16: he abstains from accepting women and girls,
mn38:33.17: he abstains from accepting male and female slaves,
mn38:33.18: he abstains from accepting goats and sheep,
mn38:33.19: he abstains from accepting chickens and pigs,
mn38:33.2: Giving up taking what is not given, he abstains from taking what is not given; taking only what is given, expecting only what is given, he dwells pure in himself by non-stealing.
mn38:33.20: he abstains from accepting elephants, cattle, horses, and mares,
mn38:33.21: he abstains from accepting fields and land,
mn38:33.22: he abstains from running errands and carrying messages,
mn38:33.23: he abstains from buying and selling,
mn38:33.24: he abstains from false weights, false metals, and false measures,
mn38:33.25: he abstains from cheating, deceiving, fraud, and trickery,
mn38:33.26: he abstains from wounding, killing, imprisoning, robbery, plunder, and violence.
mn38:33.3: Giving up the non-holy life, he lives the holy life, living apart, abstaining from the village practice of sexual intercourse.
mn38:33.4: Giving up false speech, he abstains from false speech; he speaks truth, is devoted to truth, reliable, trustworthy, not a deceiver of the world.
mn38:33.5: Giving up divisive speech, he abstains from divisive speech—what he hears here he does not repeat elsewhere to divide those people from these, and what he hears there he does not repeat to these to divide these people from those. Thus he is a reconciler of the divided, a promoter of the united, one who delights in harmony, rejoices in harmony, is glad in harmony, and speaks words that make for harmony.
mn38:33.6: Giving up harsh speech, he abstains from harsh speech—he speaks words that are gentle, pleasing to the ear, affectionate, going to the heart, courteous, desired by many, agreeable to many.
mn38:33.7: Giving up idle chatter, he abstains from idle chatter; he speaks at the right time, speaks what is real, speaks what is beneficial, speaks *dhamma*, speaks *vinaya*; at the right time he speaks words worth treasuring, reasonable, measured, connected with the goal.
mn38:33.8: He abstains from damaging seeds and plant life,
mn38:33.9: he eats one meal a day, abstaining at night, refraining from eating at improper times.
mn38:34.1: He is content with robes to protect the body and almsfood to sustain the belly. Wherever he goes, he sets out taking only these with him.
mn38:34.2: Just as a bird, wherever it flies, flies with its wings as its only burden,
mn38:34.3: so too a bhikkhu is content with robes to protect the body and almsfood to sustain the belly. Wherever he goes, he sets out taking only these with him.
mn38:34.4: Possessing this noble aggregate of ethical conduct, he experiences within himself a blameless sukha.
mn38:35.1: Having seen a form with the eye, he does not grasp at its signs or features.
mn38:35.2: Since, if he were to dwell with the eye *indriya* unguarded [*cakkhundriyaṁ*], evil unskillful *dhammas* of longing and distress might overwhelm him, he practices for its restraint, guards the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*], and undertakes restraint of the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriye saṁvaraṁ*].
mn38:35.3: Having heard a sound with the ear …
mn38:35.4: having smelled an odor with the nose …
mn38:35.5: having tasted a flavor with the tongue …
mn38:35.6: having touched a tangible with the body …
mn38:35.7: having cognized a *dhamma* with the mind, he does not grasp at its signs or features.
mn38:35.8: Since, if he were to dwell with the mind *indriya* unguarded [*manindriyaṁ*], evil unskillful *dhammas* of longing and distress might overwhelm him, he practices for its restraint, guards the mind *indriya* [*manindriyaṁ*], and undertakes restraint of the mind *indriya* [*manindriye saṁvaraṁ*].
mn38:35.9: Possessing this noble restraint of the *indriyas* [*indriyasaṁvarena*], he experiences within himself a non-leaking sukha.
mn38:36.1: He acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when going forward and returning; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when looking ahead and looking away; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when bending and stretching; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when wearing his robe and carrying his bowl; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when eating, drinking, consuming food, and tasting; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when defecating and urinating; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when walking, standing, sitting, sleeping, waking, speaking, and keeping silent.
mn38:37.1: Possessing this noble aggregate of ethical conduct, possessing this noble contentment, possessing this noble restraint of the *indriyas* [*indriyasaṁvarena*], and possessing this noble *sati* and full awareness [*satisampajaññena*],
mn38:37.2: he resorts to a secluded resting place: a forest, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a hillside cave, a charnel ground, a woodland grove, an open space, or a heap of straw.
mn38:38.1: After the meal, on returning from the almsround, he sits down cross-legged, setting his body erect, and establishing *sati* before him.
mn38:38.2: Giving up covetousness for the world, he dwells with a mind free of covetousness, purifying the mind of covetousness;
mn38:38.3: giving up ill will and resentment, he dwells with a mind free of ill will, compassionate for the welfare of all living beings, purifying the mind of ill will and resentment;
mn38:38.4: giving up dullness and drowsiness, he dwells free from dullness and drowsiness, percipient of light, with *sati*, with full awareness [*sampajāno*], purifying the mind of dullness and drowsiness;
mn38:38.5: giving up restlessness and remorse, he dwells unagitated, with an inwardly settled mind [*vūpasantacitto*], purifying the mind of restlessness and remorse;
mn38:38.6: giving up doubt, he dwells having crossed over doubt, without uncertainty regarding skillful *dhammas*, purifying the mind of doubt.
mn38:39.1: Having given up these five hindrances, corruptions of the mind that weaken discernment [*paññā*],
mn38:39.2: quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful *dhammas*, he enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with pīti and sukha born of seclusion [*viveka*].
mn38:39.3: Furthermore, bhikkhus, with the settling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with inner clarity, with unification [*ekodibhāvaṁ*] of mind, he enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*, without thought [*vitakka*] and without examination [*vicāra*], with pīti and sukha born of *samādhi* [*samādhijaṁ*] …
mn38:39.5: he enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
mn38:4.1: Since those bhikkhus were unable to draw the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, away from this harmful view, they went to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone to the *Bhagavā*, they paid homage to him and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, those bhikkhus said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn38:4.10: ‘That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, not another.’
mn38:4.11: Then, bhante, wanting to draw the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, away from this harmful view, we questioned him, pressed him, and spoke to him:
mn38:4.12: ‘Do not say that, friend Sāti. Do not misrepresent the *Bhagavā*, for it is not good to misrepresent the *Bhagavā*. The *Bhagavā* would not speak in that way.
mn38:4.13: In many ways, friend Sāti, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] has been spoken of by the *Bhagavā* as *paṭiccasamuppāda*'ed [*paṭiccasamuppannaṁ*]; apart from a condition, there is no arising of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].’
mn38:4.14: But even though the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, was being questioned, pressed, and spoken to by us, he still kept insisting on that very harmful view, grasping at it stubbornly and clinging to it:
mn38:4.15: ‘That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, not another.’
mn38:4.16: Since we were unable to draw the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, away from this harmful view, we reported this matter to the *Bhagavā*.”
mn38:4.2: “Bhante, for the bhikkhu named Sāti, the fisherman’s son, this harmful view has arisen:
mn38:4.3: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, not another.’
mn38:4.4: We heard, bhante, that for the bhikkhu named Sāti, the fisherman’s son, this harmful view had arisen:
mn38:4.5: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, not another.’
mn38:4.6: Then, bhante, we went to the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son. Having gone to him, we said this to him:
mn38:4.7: ‘Is it true, friend Sāti, that this harmful view has arisen for you:
mn38:4.8: “As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, not another”?’
mn38:4.9: When this was said, bhante, the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, said this to us:
mn38:40.1: Having seen a form with the eye, he does not lust after an attractive form, nor is he repelled by an unattractive form; and he dwells with *sati* of the body established [*upaṭṭhitakāyasati*], with a boundless mind.
mn38:40.2: And he understands as it truly is that liberation of mind and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*] where those evil unskillful *dhammas* cease without remainder for him.
mn38:40.3: Having thus abandoned favoring and opposing, whatever *vedanā* he feels—pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant—he does not delight in that *vedanā*, does not welcome it, and does not remain holding to it.
mn38:40.4: As he does not delight in that *vedanā*, does not welcome it, and does not remain holding to it, whatever delight there is in *vedanās* ceases.
mn38:40.5: With the cessation of delight there is the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*]; with the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*], the cessation of *bhava*; with the cessation of *bhava*, the cessation of birth; with the cessation of birth, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, distress, and despair cease.
mn38:40.6: That is how there is the cessation of this whole mass of *dukkha*.
mn38:41.1: Having heard a sound with the ear …
mn38:41.10: That is how there is the cessation of this whole mass of *dukkha*.
mn38:41.11: Bhikkhus, understand from me in brief this liberation through the destruction of *taṇhā*; but the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, is ensnared in the great net of *taṇhā*, caught in the massed tangle of *taṇhā*.”
mn38:41.13: Satisfied, those bhikkhus rejoiced in the *Bhagavā*’s words.
mn38:41.14: The Greater Discourse on the Destruction of *Taṇhā* is finished, the eighth.
mn38:41.2: having smelled an odor with the nose …
mn38:41.3: having tasted a flavor with the tongue …
mn38:41.4: having touched a tangible with the body …
mn38:41.5: having cognized a *dhamma* with the mind, he does not lust after an attractive *dhamma*, nor is he repelled by an unattractive *dhamma*; and he dwells with *sati* of the body established [*upaṭṭhitakāyasati*], with a boundless mind,
mn38:41.6: and he understands as it truly is that liberation of mind and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*] where those evil unskillful *dhammas* cease without remainder for him.
mn38:41.7: Having thus abandoned favoring and opposing, whatever *vedanā* he feels—pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant—he does not delight in that *vedanā*, does not welcome it, and does not remain holding to it.
mn38:41.8: As he does not delight in that *vedanā*, does not welcome it, and does not remain holding to it, whatever delight there is in *vedanās* ceases.
mn38:41.9: With the cessation of delight there is the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*]; with the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*], the cessation of *bhava*; with the cessation of *bhava*, the cessation of birth; with the cessation of birth, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, distress, and despair cease.
mn38:5.1: Then the *Bhagavā* addressed a certain bhikkhu:
mn38:5.11: “Bhante, it is that which speaks and feels, which experiences here and there the result of good and bad kammas.”
mn38:5.12: “What now, foolish man, do you understand to have been taught by me in that way?
mn38:5.13: Have I not, foolish man, in many ways spoken of consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as *paṭiccasamuppāda*'ed [*paṭiccasamuppannaṁ*], and that apart from a condition there is no arising of consciousness [*viññāṇa*]?
mn38:5.14: But you, foolish man, by your own wrong grasping, both misrepresent us, wound yourself, and generate much demerit.
mn38:5.15: For that will lead to your harm and dukkha for a long time.”
mn38:5.2: “Come, bhikkhu, in my name address the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son:
mn38:5.4: “Yes, bhante,” that bhikkhu replied to the *Bhagavā*. Then he went to the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, and said to him:
mn38:5.6: “Yes, friend,” the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, replied to that bhikkhu. Then he went to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone to the *Bhagavā*, he paid homage to him and sat down to one side. When the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, was sitting to one side, the *Bhagavā* said this to him:
mn38:5.7: “Is it true, Sāti, that this harmful view has arisen for you:
mn38:5.8: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, not another’?”
mn38:5.9: “That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, bhante: it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, not another.”
mn38:6.3: is this bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, even warmed up in this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*]?”
mn38:6.6: When this was said, the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, sat silent, dismayed, shoulders drooping, downcast, brooding, and without a reply.
mn38:6.7: Then the *Bhagavā*, knowing that the bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, was silent, dismayed, shoulders drooping, downcast, brooding, and without a reply, said this to him:
mn38:6.8: “You will be exposed, foolish man, by this evil view of your own.
mn38:6.9: Now I shall question the bhikkhus here.”
mn38:7.2: “Bhikkhus, do you too understand the *dhamma* taught by me in the way that this bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, by his own wrong grasping both misrepresents us, wounds himself, and generates much demerit?”
mn38:7.4: For in many ways, bhante, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] has been spoken of to us by the *Bhagavā* as *paṭiccasamuppāda*'ed [*paṭiccasamuppannaṁ*]; apart from a condition there is no arising of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].”
mn38:7.6: It is good, bhikkhus, that you understand the *dhamma* taught by me in that way.
mn38:7.7: For in many ways, bhikkhus, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] has been spoken of by me to you as *paṭiccasamuppāda*'ed [*paṭiccasamuppannaṁ*]; apart from a condition there is no arising of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn38:7.8: But this bhikkhu Sāti, the fisherman’s son, by his own wrong grasping both misrepresents us, wounds himself, and generates much demerit.
mn38:7.9: For that will lead to the harm and dukkha of that foolish man for a long time.
mn38:8.1: Whatever condition, bhikkhus, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises dependent on, by that very thing it is reckoned.
mn38:8.10: Dependent on splinters, a fire burns; it is reckoned simply as a splinter fire.
mn38:8.11: Dependent on grass, a fire burns; it is reckoned simply as a grass fire.
mn38:8.12: Dependent on cow-dung, a fire burns; it is reckoned simply as a cow-dung fire.
mn38:8.13: Dependent on husks, a fire burns; it is reckoned simply as a husk fire.
mn38:8.14: Dependent on rubbish, a fire burns; it is reckoned simply as a rubbish fire.
mn38:8.15: Just so, bhikkhus, whatever condition consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises dependent on, by that very thing it is reckoned.
mn38:8.16: Dependent on the eye and forms, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*].
mn38:8.17: Dependent on the ear and sounds, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as ear-consciousness [*sotaviññāṇaṁ*].
mn38:8.18: Dependent on the nose and smells, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as nose-consciousness [*ghānaviññāṇaṁ*].
mn38:8.19: Dependent on the tongue and tastes, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as tongue-consciousness [*jivhāviññāṇaṁ*].
mn38:8.2: Dependent on the eye and forms, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*].
mn38:8.20: Dependent on the body and tangibles, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as body-consciousness [*kāyaviññāṇaṁ*].
mn38:8.21: Dependent on the mind and *dhammas*, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*].
mn38:8.3: Dependent on the ear and sounds, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as ear-consciousness [*sotaviññāṇaṁ*].
mn38:8.4: Dependent on the nose and smells, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as nose-consciousness [*ghānaviññāṇaṁ*].
mn38:8.5: Dependent on the tongue and tastes, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as tongue-consciousness [*jivhāviññāṇaṁ*].
mn38:8.6: Dependent on the body and tangibles, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as body-consciousness [*kāyaviññāṇaṁ*].
mn38:8.7: Dependent on the mind and *dhammas*, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*].
mn38:8.8: Suppose, bhikkhus, that whatever condition a fire burns dependent on, by that very thing it is reckoned.
mn38:8.9: Dependent on wood, a fire burns; it is reckoned simply as a wood fire.
mn38:9.1: “Do you see, bhikkhus, ‘This has come to be’?”
mn38:9.3: “Do you see, bhikkhus, ‘This has come to be from nutriment’?”
mn38:9.5: “Do you see, bhikkhus, ‘With the cessation of that nutriment, what has come to be is of a nature to cease’?”
